serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chao Long Dac Biet is approximately 350 g (one bowl), containing a generous portion of rice porridge with mixed pork organs, blood curd, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Special Vietnamese Pork Organ Porridge (Chao Long Dac Biet), cr_id G00010528, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice congee / rice porridge analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chao Long Dac Biet. GI estimated at 78 (high) based on rice congee / rice porridge analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which report GI values of 78–88 for white rice congee/porridge. The carbohydrate_g per 100g is reported as 0.0 by HPB, which likely reflects the organ-meat-dominant fraction of the dish analysed; however, the dish contains a white rice porridge base with a high GI. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100. Because the HPB-reported carbohydrate for this record is 0.0g, GL per serving computes to 0.0; this is a known limitation of the HPB record and is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold — driven by fish sauce, seasoned pork bone stock, and pork blood curd.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request preparation with reduced fish sauce and no added salt, limit portion to no more than 150 g, and avoid additional condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice porridge base carries a high glycemic index (estimated GI ~78–88); prolonged cooking of rice further gelatinises starch, accelerating glucose absorption and causing rapid postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should minimise or avoid this dish; if consumed, limit portion size to a small bowl, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and request brown rice or multi-grain congee substitution where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 89 mg/100 g from pork organs (liver, intestines, heart, blood curd), combined with high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) and likely saturated fat from organ meats, presents a significant cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its high cholesterol, high sodium, and organ-meat saturated fat content; if consumed occasionally, restrict to a very small portion and avoid high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork organs are rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium worsens RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pork blood curd is also high in purines, which is particularly relevant when RA co-exists with gout.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cháo Lòng Đặc Biệt (Special Vietnamese Pork Organ Porridge) is a traditional Vietnamese rice porridge dish featuring a rich assortment of pork offal including intestines, liver, stomach, heart, lung, and pork blood curd, slow-simmered in a seasoned pork bone broth. The dish is served over or mixed with soft white rice congee and garnished with spring onion, fried shallots, and fresh ginger. The texture is silky and thick from the slow-cooked rice, with tender, richly flavoured organ meats providing a savoury, umami-forward taste profile. Fish sauce and pork bone stock contribute a deeply savoury, slightly salty broth. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found in Vietnamese food stalls and hawker centres, typically consumed at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g — the near-zero carbohydrate figure reflects the organ-meat-dominant fraction analysed, though the dish contains a white rice porridge base. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100 g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid-retention conditions. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100 g from the organ meats. The dish is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI white rice congee base, nor for those with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork organs.

notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this record. This is confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010528). The HPB record appears to reflect the organ-meat-dominant fraction of the dish; however, the dish contains a white rice porridge base. This is a known limitation of the HPB record and users should be aware that the carbohydrate content of the full dish as consumed is likely higher than 0.0 g/100 g. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese pork organ porridge preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010528 as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per serving is computed as 0.0 because HPB reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 for this record; this is a known data limitation. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on white rice congee entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). This dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork and pork blood products.
